## The Meaning of the Propositional Connectives

### Truth Tables

Our next task is to explain what makes an argument in the language L$_P$ a valid
argument. Just as before, the idea of argumentative validity will be expressed
in terms of necessary truth preservation. An argument is valid just in case the
truth of the conclusion follows necessarily from that of the premises. Moreover,
the relevant notion of necessity will still be made out in semantic terms. An
argument is valid just in case there is no interpretation of the non-logical
elements of the language under which the premises are true and the conclusion
false.

What are the non-logical elements of the language L$_P$? In this case, they are
the atoms. These, naturally, are meant to stand for the sort of simple
propositions out of which compound propositions are constructed. So, an argument
is valid just in case the truth of the conclusion follows from that of the
premises, no matter what propositions the atoms stand for.

But what exactly is a proposition? From the perspective of the general
philosophy of language, this, of course, is a complicated question. But from the
point of view of truth-functional propositional logic, it admits of a fairly
straightforward answer. In the context of logic, what is important about an
interpretation of a language is that it serves to underwrite a specific truth valuation of the sentences
of the language. Since we are dealing exclusively with truth-functional
operations, the truth values of compound sentences will be fully determined by
the truth values of the component sentences out of which they are formed. In
this way, the truth values of all the sentences of the language will supervene
on the truth values of the atomic ones.

So, while the atomic sentences do represent simple propositions, the only
feature of these propositions that is semantically relevant in the sense that
it contributes to assessing the truth values of others sentences in the
language, is their own truth or falsehood. Thus, in this context, we may think
of a simple proposition as nothing more than the bearer of a truth value, and
the possible semantic values of the atoms as the possible truth values of the
propositions they express. The only assumptions we will make about these simple
propositions are (1) that each is _logically contingent_ in the sense that each
is a proposition that may be either true or false; and (2) that they are
_logically independent_ of each other in the sense that the truth or falsehood
of any one such proposition imposes no constraints on the truth or falsehood of
any other.

This means that, we will include among our interpretations of the
language all possible assignments of truth values to the atoms. We thus arrive
at the following definition:

:::{#def-p-interpretation .definition}
An _interpretation_ of the language L$_P$ is a function $\sigma$ which assigns
to each of the atoms a truth value, i.e., an object from the set $\{\true,
  \false\}$.
:::

To complete our specification of the semantics for the language, we must explain
how a given interpretation $\si$ determines a unique truth valuation $v^\si$ for the
language. In doing so, we will, in effect, be explaining the meanings of the
logical elements of the syntax, that is, the propositional connectives.

For the atomic sentences, the truth value of a sentence is given directly by the
interpretation as follows:

(Atomic Sentences):
If $\ph$ is an atom, then $v^\si(\ph)=\si(\ph)$.[^atom]

[^atom]:
    Strictly speaking, the occurrence of '$\ph$' that appears as an
    argument of $v^\si$ stands for an atomic sentence, whereas the occcurence
    of '$\ph$' that appears as an argument of $\si$ stands for an atom. However,
    for ease of exposition, we will generally treat as identical an atomic
    sentence and the atom from which it is composed.

To assign truth values to compound sentences, we must introduce additional
semantic rules, specifying the meaning of the propositional connectives. We
do so with the aim of interpreting the propositional connectives as effecting
roughly the same semantic operations as the ordinary English locutions to which
they correspond in @fig-connectives.

Let's begin with negation. We have already said that the truth-functional
operation effected by negation is that of transforming one sentence into another
sentence that is true just in case the original sentence is false. This can be
captured by the following rule:

(Negation):
For any sentence $\ph$: $v^\si(\neg\ph)=\true$ iff
$v^\si(\ph)=\false$.

Notice the 'iff' here. Since we are still assuming that every sentence is either
true or false, and that no sentence is both, it follows from our rule for
negation that if $v^\si(\ph)=\true$, then $v^\si(\neg\ph)=\false$.

Now, let's consider conjunction. Again, as we've already said the
truth-functional operation of conjunction takes two sentences and returns a new
sentence which is true iff both of its component sentences---or _conjuncts_---are true. We can describe
this operation as follows:

(Conjunction):
For any sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$: $v^\si((\ph\conj\psi))=\true$ iff
$v^\si(\ph)=\true$ and $v^\si(\psi)=\true$.

With the rules for negation and conjunction in place, we can now consider the
truth conditions for more complicated compound sentences, such as $\neg(P\conj
\neg Q)$. How does the truth value of this sentence depend upon the truth values
of the atoms $P$ and $Q$? By the rule for negation, the
sentence $\neg(P\conj\neg Q)$ is true iff the sentence $(P\conj\neg Q)$ is
false. By the rule for conjunction, $(P\conj\neg Q)$ is false iff either $P$ is
false or $\neg Q$ is false (or both). Since $\neg Q$ is false iff $Q$ is true, the whole
sentence $\neg(P\conj\neg Q)$ is true iff either $P$ is false or $Q$ is true (or
both).

One particularly convenient format for expressing the truth conditions for
compound sentences is called a truth table. In a truth table, each of the atoms
appearing in the sentence is assigned a separate column and each row
corresponds to a different possible assignment of truth values to these atoms.
The final column in the table then specifies the truth values of the compound
sentence in question for each interpretation of the atoms. For example, a truth table
for the sentence $\neg(P\conj \neg Q)$ is as follows:

|   $P$    |   $Q$    | $\neg(P\conj \neg Q)$ |
| :------: | :------: | :-------------------: |
| $\true$  | $\true$  |        $\true$        |
| $\true$  | $\false$ |       $\false$        |
| $\false$ | $\true$  |        $\true$        |
| $\false$ | $\false$ |        $\true$        |

Note that this truth table has four rows. That is because the sentence in
question contains two atoms. Since each atom can take one of two truth values,
in order to countenance all possible interpretations, we need
$2\times 2 = 4$ rows. If we considered a sentence containing three distinct
atoms, we would need a truth table with $2 \times 2\times 2 = 8$ rows. In
general, a sentence with $n$ distinct atoms requires a truth table with $2^n$
rows.

When populating the initial columns in a truth table, it is important to ensure
that all possible interpretations of the atoms are countenanced. The most
straightforward way to ensure that this is the case is to enumerate the truth
values of the atoms in accordance with the following systematic procedure: in
the first column, fill in the first half of the rows with $\true$ and the second
half with $\false$. In the second column, fill in the first quarter of the rows
with $\true$, the second quarter with $\false$, the third quarter with $\true$,
and the last quarter with $\false$. Continue in this way, writing down
alternating blocks of $\true$ and $\false$ and at each new column cutting the size
of the blocks in half, until you reach the last column for an atom
in which $\true$ and $\false$ alternate row by row.

### Logical Equivalence

Now, let's return to our specification of the meanings of the propositional
connectives. Disjunction corresponds to the English language operation 'either
... or ---'. Now, in natural language there exists a certain ambiguity in how we
interpret such expressions. Sometimes we interpret these expressions
'exclusively' as meaning that one of the two component sentences is true, but not
both. For instance:

Either you may have coffee or you may have tea.

In other cases, we interpret disjunctions 'inclusively' as meaning that at least
one, and possibly both, of the component sentences are true, as in:

Either the train is late or the schedule posted online is wrong.

In specifying the meaning of the disjunction operator in the language L$_P$, we
will assume that it signifies inclusive disjunction, and hence that a
disjunction is true just in case either (or both) of its component
sentences---or, _disjuncts_---is true:

(Disjunction):
For any sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$: $v^\si((\ph\conj\psi))=\true$ iff
$v^\si(\ph)=\true$ or $v^\si(\psi)=\true$ (or both).

The truth table for the sentence $P\vee Q$ is as follows:

|   $P$    |   $Q$    | $P\vee Q$ |
| :------: | :------: | :-------: |
| $\true$  | $\true$  |  $\true$  |
| $\true$  | $\false$ |  $\true$  |
| $\false$ | $\true$  |  $\true$  |
| $\false$ | $\false$ | $\false$  |

:::{#exr-exclusive-or .exercise}

{{< include /content/exercises/exclusive-or.qmd >}}

:::

We are now in a position to understand why we needed to introduce parentheses
into our language of propositional logic. Suppose we omitted the parentheses and
allowed for unparenthesized sentences such as $P\conj Q\vee R$. As we observed
in @sec-induction, this sentence would have two distinct formation trees. What
the semantic rules for conjunction and disjunction tell us is, in effect, how
truth values assigned to the terminal nodes of such a tree, labeled by atoms,
are transmitted up through the tree to the compound sentence labeling the root
node of the tree, in which those atoms figure. It would, of course, be a bad
thing if some assignment of truth values to the atoms, transmits to the root
node of one of the formation trees for a sentence a truth value that is
different from that which it transmits to the root node of another formation
tree for that same sentence. For in this case, the rules would generate an
inconsistency, implying that the sentence is both true and false.

It turns out that this sort of incoherency would be possible if we omitted
parentheses from the language. For example, let
$\si(P)=\false$, $\si(Q)=\true$, and $\si(R)=\true$. Then, transmitting these
values up through the two formation trees for the sentence $P\conj Q\vee R$ in
accordance with the rules for conjunction and disjunction results in different
truth values being assigned to this sentence. Following one tree it is assigned
the value false:

![](/content/static/images/noparen-tree-1-tv.svg){width=250 fig-align="center" fig-alt="sentences
formation tree"}

But following the other it is assigned the value true:

![](/content/static/images/noparen-tree-2-tv.svg){width=250 fig-align="center" fig-alt="sentences formation tree"}

To avoid this sort of semantic incoherence, we need our language to distinguish
between these two sentences. This is why we introduced parentheses into our
language. By means of parentheses, we enforce a strict 'order of operations'
which ensures that no sentence admits of multiple distinct formation trees. In
this way, we disambiguate the sentence $P\conj Q\vee R$, associating its first
formation tree with the sentence $(P\conj (Q\vee R))$ and its second formation tree with the
sentence $((P\conj Q)\vee R)$.

Now, that we have better understood the need for parentheses, we can be a bit
more lax in our use of parentheses for the sake of readability. In particular,
when writing sentences, we will omit the outermost pair of parentheses. We will
also assume an order of operations for the propositional connectives that will
allow us to express sentences using fewer parentheses than are strictly required
by the grammatical rules of L$_P$. In accordance with this order of operations,
negations are to be parenthesized first, in as narrow a manner as possible, then
conjunctions and disjunctions, then conditionals and, finally, biconditionals.
Thus, for example, to express the sentence: $$ ((\neg P\conj Q)\imp
\neg R) $$ we may write: $$ \neg P \conj Q \imp \neg R $$

:::{#exr-polish .exercise}

{{< include /content/exercises/polish.qmd >}}

:::

Let's now consider the conditional operator. This operator is meant to model the
English language construction 'if ..., then ---'. But of all the connectives, it is however, perhaps the
farthest removed from its natural language counterpart. This is because
conditional constructions are used to express a variety of different
relationships between sentences, most of which are not truth-functional. For
example:

$$
\textrm{If the match is struck, then it will light.}
$$

This conditional expresses a causal relationship, and so, as we have already
noted, its truth value does not depend on the truth value of its component
sentences---referred to as its _antecedent_ and _consequent_, respectively.

So, how should we think of a conditional in strictly truth-functional
terms? To begin with, we may assume that a conditional is true if both its
antecedent and consequent are true. Moreover, we may assume that it is false if
its antecedent is true and its consequent false. The harder cases to handle are
those in which the antecedent is false. What truth value should we assign to the
conditional in these cases? We will adopt the convention, odd as it may seem,
that a conditional with a false antecedent is true. As we will see later, there
is a proof theoretic rationale for this policy.

So, the semantic rule for conditionals is as follows:

(Conditional):
For any sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$: $v^\si(\ph\imp\psi)=\false$ iff
$v^\si(\ph)=\true$ and $v^\si(\psi)=\false$.

The truth table for $P\imp Q$ is as follows:

|   $P$    |   $Q$    | $P\imp Q$ |
| :------: | :------: | :-------: |
| $\true$  | $\true$  |  $\true$  |
| $\true$  | $\false$ | $\false$  |
| $\false$ | $\true$  |  $\true$  |
| $\false$ | $\false$ |  $\true$  |

Here is a fact about the conditional that is worth noting:

$$\Ga, \ph\models\psi\textrm{ iff }\Ga\models\ph\imp\psi$$

The claim $\Ga,\ph\models\psi$ is true iff there is no interpretation under
which every sentence in $\Ga\cup\{\ph\}$ is true and $\psi$ is false. This just
means that there is no interpretation under which every sentence in $\Ga$ is
true, $\ph$ is true, and $\psi$ is false. But, given the meaning of the
conditional, this is equivalent to the claim that there is no
interpretation under which every sentence in $\Ga$ is true and $\ph\imp \psi$ is
false, i.e., $\Ga\models\ph\imp\psi$.

Notice that the truth conditions for the conditional $P\imp Q$ are exactly the
same as the truth conditions for the sentence $\neg(P\conj \neg Q)$. Both are
true just in case either $P$ is false or $Q$ is true (or both). This highlights
the fact that the syntax of the language L$_P$ now greatly exceeds its
expressive capacity. That is, in the language, there are many distinct ways of
saying the same thing.

When two sentences have exactly the same truth conditions, we say that they are
_logically equivalent_.

:::{#def-logical-equivalence .definition}
Two sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$ are logically equivalent, written $\ph\equiv
\psi$, if, for any
interpretation $\si$, $v^\si(\ph)=v^\si(\psi)$.
:::

:::{#exr-truth-tables .exercise}
{{< include /content/exercises/truth-tables.qmd >}}
:::

Two other important semantic notions are those of logical truth and logical falsehood:

:::{#def-logical-equivalence .definition}
A sentence $\ph$ is a _logical truth_ if, for every interpretation $\si$:
$v^\si(\ph)=\true$. A sentence $\ph$ is a _logical falsehood_ if, for every interpretation $\si$:
$v^\si(\ph)=\false$.
:::

In the context of propositional logic, logical truths are sometimes referred to
as _tautologies_ and logical falsehoods as _contradictions_. In fact, our
final connective, the biconditional, allows us to express logical equivalence in
terms of logical truth. The rule for the biconditional is as follows:

(Biconditional):
For any sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$: $v^\si(\ph\iff\psi)=\true$ iff
$v^\si(\ph)=v^\si(\psi)$.

Convince yourself that, for any sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$, $\ph\equiv\psi$ iff
the sentence $\ph\iff\psi$ is a tautology.

Now, let's see how biconditionals can be utilized in ordinary reasoning. First, watch the following
clip from the 1986 film _Labryinth_:

::: {#fig-labyrinth .figure}
{{< video /content/static/video/labyrinth.mp4 >}}
The Puzzle of the Two Doors. Clip from _Labyrinth_ (1986), directed by Jim Henson. © TriStar Pictures / Henson Associates / Lucasfilm Ltd. Used for commentary and analysis.
:::

In the scene depicted, the heroine is confronted with a logical puzzle: she must
choose between two doors, one of which leads to the castle and the other to
certain death. In front of one of the two doors is a red guard and in front of
the other is a blue guard. The heroine knows that one of the guards always tells
the truth and the other always lies. She is allowed to ask one of the guards a
question, and so she asks the red guard what the blue guard would say if he were asked
where the door behind the red guard leads. The red guard replies that the
blue guard would say that this door leads to the castle. Based on this response,
the heroine (rightly) concludes that it is, in fact, the door behind the blue
guard that leads to the castle.

How do we formulate the heroine's argument? The first premise of the argument is
that one of the guards always tells the truth and the other guard always lies. Let:

$R$: The red guard is the truth-teller

$B$: The blue guard is the truth-teller

The premise states that one of these claims is true and the other is false. This
can be expressed by the sentence:

$$ R\iff \neg B$$

When asked, the red guard tells the heroine that the blue guard would report that
the door behind the red guard leads to the castle. To say that this is what the
blue guard reports is tantamount to saying that the blue guard speaks the truth
just in case the door behind the red guard leads to the castle. Let:

$C$: The door behind the red guard leads the castle

So, the fact that the blue guard reports this can be captured by the biconditional:

$$ B\iff C $$

Now, the Heroine is not yet in a position to conclude that the blue guard will
actually say what the red guard says that he will say---that the door behind the
red guard leads to the castle---since it may be the case that the red guard is
lying. But what she does know is that the red guard is telling the truth if and
only if this is what the blue guard will say. That is:

$$ R\iff (B\iff C) $$

From these two assumptions, the heroine concludes that it is not the door behind
the red guard, but the door behind the blue guard, that leads to the castle. We
can validate the heroine's argument by means of the following truth table:

|   $R$    |   $B$    |   $C$    | $R\iff\neg B$ | $R\iff(B\iff C)$ |
| :------: | :------: | :------: | :-----------: | :--------------: |
| $\true$  | $\true$  | $\true$  |   $\false$    |     $\true$      |
| $\true$  | $\true$  | $\false$ |   $\false$    |     $\false$     |
| $\true$  | $\false$ | $\true$  |    $\true$    |     $\false$     |
| $\true$  | $\false$ | $\false$ |    $\true$    |     $\true$      |
| $\false$ | $\true$  | $\true$  |    $\true$    |     $\false$     |
| $\false$ | $\true$  | $\false$ |    $\true$    |     $\true$      |
| $\false$ | $\false$ | $\true$  |   $\false$    |     $\true$      |
| $\false$ | $\false$ | $\false$ |   $\false$    |     $\false$     |

Note that the only two rows in which both assumptions are true (rows $4$ and
$6$) are rows in which $C$ is false. Hence, from these two assumptions it
follows that the door behind the blue guard leads to the castle. Propositional
logic to the rescue!

:::{#exr-dual .exercise}
{{< include /content/exercises/dual.qmd >}}
:::
